g.data-narration: Welcome to Circular Singly Linked List, an important variation where the last node connects back to the first node.
A CSLL is a linked list where the last node points to the first node instead of NULL, forming a continuous circular loop.
g.text: What is a Circular Singly Linked List?
A variation of singly linked list where the last node's
next pointer points back to the first (head) node.
Creates a closed loop enabling continuous cyclic traversal.

g.data-narration: Key features include: last node points to head, continuous loop traversal, special handling for operations, and efficient with tail pointer.
Each node contains two parts: the data field storing the value, and the next pointer referencing the subsequent node.
Here is the visual structure: three nodes with values 1, 2, and 3 where node 3 points back to node 1, creating the circle.
Compare CSLL with Singly and Doubly Linked Lists: CSLL has circular forward traversal, no NULL pointers, and moderate complexity.
To insert at beginning: create new node, traverse to last node, link new node to current head, update last node to new head.
g.text: 1. Create new node | 2. If empty, point to itself | 3. Traverse to last node

g.data-narration: To insert at end: traverse to last node, set last->next to new node, new->next points to head, head remains unchanged.
g.text: 1. Traverse to last node | 2. last->next = new | 3. new->next = head

g.data-narration: To insert after a specific node: set new->next to prev->next, then update prev->next to new. Simple two-pointer operation.
g.text: 1. Create new node | 2. new->next = prev->next | 3. prev->next = new

g.data-narration: To delete from beginning: traverse to last node, update last->next to second node, free old head, update head pointer.
g.text: 1. If single node: free and set NULL | 2. Else: traverse to last | 3. last->next = head->next

g.data-narration: To delete from end: traverse to second-last node, update its next to head, free the last node. Must track previous node.
g.text: 1. Traverse to second-last node | 2. prev->next = head | 3. Free last node

g.data-narration: To delete specific node by value: traverse tracking previous, update prev->next when found, handle head deletion specially.
g.text: 1. If head: update last->next, free head | 2. Else: traverse to find, update prev->next
do { prev = temp; temp = temp->next; } while(temp != head && temp->data != key);

g.data-narration: For traversal, use do-while loop instead of while. Process node, then check if returning to head to terminate.
g.text: Use do-while to ensure at least one iteration. Process node, move to next, terminate when back to head.
do { printf("%d ", temp->data); temp = temp->next; } while(temp != head);

g.data-narration: Applications include round-robin CPU scheduling, multiplayer game turns, circular buffers, and implementing circular queues.
Advantages: efficient cyclic traversal, round-robin ready. Disadvantages: infinite loop risk, complex operations, more error-prone.
g.text: • Efficient cyclic traversal | No NULL checks
• Infinite loop risk | Complex pointer handling

g.data-narration: Exam tips: remember last points to first, use do-while for traversal, handle edge cases like empty and single-node lists.
